name: Residential Environment vs. Clinical Monitoring Trade-Off
consequences: Immediate: The facility prioritizes domestic ambiance (soft lighting, naturalistic acoustics, unrestricted mobility) over sterile clinical controls, which increases parasomnia event manifestation but reduces technician ability to intervene rapidly. Systemic: Event capture rate improves by an estimated 15–25% due to fewer suppression effects, but mean response time to unsafe behavior extends from <2 min (clinical unit) to 4–6 min, increasing liability exposure and potentially requiring expanded night staffing by 1–2 FTE. Strategic: This trade-off directly impacts Aim 1 validation—operationalizing a safe, ethics-approved model requires demonstrating that domestic ambiance does not undermine incident response or legal defensibility, fundamentally shaping the scalability logic for expansion to 12 suites.
options: Maintain current design with softened clinical elements (padded edges, muted lighting) and accept 4–6 min response times, validated by a pre-study safety simulation protocol and a liability review by University Hospital Bonn's legal and risk teams.

Introduce a hybrid monitoring model with ambient presence—a trained overnight attendant stationed in an open common area rather than isolated technician rooms, enabling <3 min response times while preserving subjective domesticity through shared living-room décor and minimal visible surveillance.

Deploy a rapid-escalation architecture with wearable actigraphy triggers that alert a remote sleep physician at University Hospital Bonn (located 15 km away) when motion patterns suggest imminent high-risk events, enabling pre-event intervention via intercom with on-site trained paramedic backup on 24-min standby.
review: Controls domestic behavioral authenticity vs. institutional safety obligation. Weakness: The options fail to consider the psychological impact of an attendant's presence on participant self-consciousness and naturalistic sleep-stage progression, nor do they address whether a remote physician model meets German medical liability standards for residential research.

name: Recruitment Funnel Efficiency and Event Yield Optimization
consequences: Immediate: The current pre-admission pathway (clinical interview, collateral history, prior PSG, exclusion checklist) reliably filters out epilepsy mimics and unsafe candidates but admits only 18–22 participants annually at current University Hospital Bonn clinic volume, consuming 40–50% of operational capacity (year 1: 15–20 admissions; year 2: 30–35 admissions). Systemic: Broadening the funnel to include regional neurologist referrals and Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network cohorts could increase annual yield to 35–45 but risks a 5–8% increase in unproductive admissions (zero captured events) and escalates inter-rater reliability burden by ~200 scoring hours per year, reducing year 2 publication velocity. Strategic: Recruitment strategy directly constrains Aim 2 power—if event capture remains at the 70–80% success rate baseline, 50–70 total participants yields only 35–56 adjudicated events, which may be insufficient for robust between-person phenotypic clustering; accelerating recruitment increases data completeness but dilutes screening rigor and strains the 3-FTE technician model.
options: Restrict recruitment exclusively to University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic and established regional neurology referral networks with historical >85% pre-screening accuracy, ensuring high-confidence inclusion but accepting slower enrollment (30–35 year-2 admissions) and deferring expansion planning to post-publication phase.

Implement a two-tier admission structure: fast-track pathway for high-confidence referrals (existing clinic patients) admitted for 2–4 week baseline stays, plus an extended-screening cohort admitted for 4–8 week observation with adaptive protocol (triggered PSG only after one captured event, reducing initial resource demand), enabling 40–45 annual admissions.

Launch a community-facing recruitment campaign partnering with Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin chapters, online patient registries, and general practitioner networks, coupled with a remote pre-screening questionnaire and home-actigraphy screening phase, targeting 50+ annual admissions but requiring a dedicated 0.5-FTE recruitment coordinator and accepting a 12–15% unproductive-admission ceiling.
review: Controls enrollment velocity vs. screening fidelity and technician workload. Weakness: The options fail to account for seasonal variation in parasomnia presentation, the role of comorbid insomnia in participant retention and compliance, and whether extended stays (8–10 weeks) are financially or ethically sustainable given the €80/night compensation and participant burden.

name: Technician Staffing Model and Event-Response Architecture
consequences: Immediate: The planned 3-FTE rotating night staff covers 8 active suites with one technician per shift, enabling ~90% real-time annotation and <8 min event response time per current design. Systemic: If event frequency assumptions prove conservative (e.g., actual events cluster to 2–3 participants per week rather than uniformly distributed), the technician cohort experiences annotation bottlenecks during high-yield weeks, accumulating a 40–80 hour backlog and delaying semi-automated triage algorithm training by 4–6 weeks; conversely, expanding to 4 FTE increases annual personnel costs by €180–220K but guarantees <150 hour monthly annotation capacity with margin. Strategic: This lever directly impacts both operational Aim 1 (sustainable safe coverage) and Aim 3 (algorithm development—cleaner, timelier annotations accelerate model training), and any staffing shortfall cascades into publication delays and quality control risks.
options: Maintain the 3-FTE rotating model with a documented maximum event-annotation backlog tolerance of 60 hours per month, and if exceeded, trigger temporary hiring of 0.5-FTE per-diem scorer (cost ~€3.5K/month) to clear backlog without permanent headcount expansion.

Shift to a 3.5-FTE core team supplemented by a dedicated clinical research assistant (0.5 FTE) positioned during peak recruitment phases (months 6–18 and 24–36), with event annotation offloaded to external trained scorers at University Hospital Bonn's sleep lab under formal inter-rater reliability protocols, at a marginal cost of €25K/year.

Implement a split-site staffing model where night technicians rotate between the Bonn facility (8 suites, one technician per shift) and University Hospital Bonn's PSG lab (supplementary scoring work), enabling schedule flexibility, cross-training on both residential and clinical PSG modalities, and reducing overtime costs by ~15%, though requiring formal inter-site scheduling infrastructure.
review: Controls real-time response capability vs. sustained annotation throughput and budget flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to address whether night technicians can reliably detect false alarms from mattress sensors and ambient microphones without clinical fatigue, or whether external scorers at the hospital meet the residential-context expertise needed for accurate event classification.

name: Data Governance and Public Sharing Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Current policy restricts video to non-shared status while depositing de-identified physiological data (EEG, EMG, actigraphy) and sensor telemetry (mattress, microphone, environment) in a public repository under data use agreements, maximizing open science but requiring a detailed privacy impact assessment and GDPR-compliant anonymization workflow adding 3–4 weeks to publication. Systemic: Adopting this model increases institutional burden by ~200 hours (legal review, de-identification scripting, metadata curation) but enables secondary use by external research groups, potentially yielding 2–3 follow-up citations and unforeseen algorithmic insights within 18–24 months; conversely, a fully closed-data model avoids privacy litigation risk but reduces scientific impact, likely limiting citations to first-author publications only (~1–2 over 3 years). Strategic: Public data availability directly shapes the long-term value of this facility—a shared dataset becomes a resource for algorithm validation (Aim 3), but video's exclusion creates a methodological asymmetry (researchers can study physiology but not behavior correlation), undermining future intervention studies and potentially weakening grant proposals for phase-two expansion.
options: Deposit only time-synchronized EEG, EOG, EMG, and actigraphy signals in a public BIDS repository (PhysioNet or Sleep-edf ecosystem), excluding mattress pressure, microphone, and all video metadata, reducing privacy review to ~1 week and enabling moderate secondary-use potential (physiology-focused machine learning).

Establish a data-use agreement framework allowing external researchers to request de-identified physiological + sensor telemetry (mattress, microphone, temperature, light) under signed academic-only license, with a 6-month approval gate managed by a data stewardship committee, enabling richer phenotyping studies but introducing governance overhead of ~50 hours/year.

Create a federated analysis model: deposit physiological core in public archives, but host video-integrated analysis tools on a University Hospital Bonn secure server accessible by approved collaborators only via institutional SSO (single sign-on), enabling external behavioral phenotyping without data export, at a one-time infrastructure cost of €25–35K plus €8K/year maintenance.
review: Controls open-science impact vs. privacy-litigation risk and governance overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether external researchers have the clinical sleep medicine expertise to interpret de-identified parasomnia events correctly, or whether a federated model can scale beyond 3–5 active collaborators without fragmenting the research narrative.

name: Semi-Automated Event-Triage Algorithm Scope and Deployment Pathway
consequences: Immediate: Current Aim 3 targets a semi-automated tool ranked against dual human scorer agreement, not autonomous diagnosis; developing a model on in-house data (years 1–2) with sensitivity and specificity reported against ground truth achieves transparent, publishable benchmarks. Systemic: A conservative sensitivity target (e.g., 80–85%) with specificity >90% requires 150–200 annotated positive events and 400+ negative-event instances; at current event-capture estimates, this demands 6–8 months of intensive data collection (year 2, months 6–14), implying that algorithm development begins month 15 at earliest, delaying Aim 3 publication to month 28–32 and compressing validation time to <4 months before final report. Strategic: The phasing of Aim 3 directly impacts phase-two funding appeal—a rapid, high-confidence proof-of-concept (sensitivity >85%, specificity >90%) published by month 28 enables a compelling narrative for an intervention-trial grant; a delayed or marginal result (published month 32 with 75–80% sensitivity) weakens the competitive position and may require an additional 12-month validation cycle, jeopardizing timeline continuity.
options: Adopt a minimal-viable-model approach: train a random-forest or gradient-boosting classifier on low-dimensional features (spectral EEG power, movement rate, microphone envelope) using 18 months of annotated data, target 80% sensitivity and 92% specificity, publish methodology and benchmarks by month 28, and explicitly scope phase-two for clinical-grade deployment with prospective external validation.

Integrate a neuroscientist collaborator from Max Planck Institute or a German university with prior parasomnia algorithm experience, initiating algorithm co-development at month 6 using transfer learning from published REM behavior-disorder detection models, aiming for 85–88% sensitivity with 94% specificity and an earlier publication timeline (month 24–26), at a cost of €80–120K in subcontract and co-author commitments.

Pursue a hybrid interpretable-AI strategy using SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) explainability layered over an ensemble model, enabling clinicians to understand why the algorithm flags an event (e.g., 'high chin EMG + arousal + acceleration spike'), reducing false-positive review burden by ~30% and supporting a phase-two narrative focused on clinical decision support rather than autonomous triage, with publication by month 26–28.
review: Controls algorithm maturity and timeline vs. validation rigor and phase-two competitiveness. Weakness: The options fail to address how the algorithm will generalize to external datasets (data from other sleep centers or future facilities) if training occurs only on Bonn-collected, highly controlled residential data with non-standard sensor suites, nor do they specify the minimum event-type diversity (terminal awakening vs. complex behavior) needed for clinically meaningful performance.

name: Equipment Redundancy and Sensor Degradation Management
consequences: Immediate: The planned dry-electrode EEG headbands and contact-free mattress sensors operate on a 12–18 month lifespan before electrode resistance increases, foam degrades, or wireless interference accumulates, necessitating mid-study replacement or recalibration. Systemic: If equipment replacement occurs during months 12–16 (peak year-2 enrollment phase), sensor switching introduces a 4–6 week validation gap to ensure new units produce equivalent signal quality; failing to validate creates a discontinuity artifact in the longitudinal dataset, potentially invalidating Aim 2 between-person comparisons and requiring post-hoc statistical harmonization that consumes 60–100 hours of data engineering time. Strategic: Equipment durability directly threatens Aim 1 operational validation—the success criterion requires 'sustained safe monitoring for at least 24 months'; if sensors fail mid-deployment without validated replacements, the facility loses continuous operation status, triggering external review (month 18 failure-trigger threshold) and jeopardizing year-3 conditional funding.
options: Procure full redundant sensor suites (second set of 8 EEG headbands, 8 mattress sensors, 4 camera units) at initial purchase (year 1, €120–150K capital), conduct quarterly calibration checks against reference PSG equipment at University Hospital Bonn, and maintain a rolling replacement schedule (replace one unit every 6 weeks), absorbing the cost upfront but guaranteeing zero downtime.

Negotiate a service-level agreement with equipment vendors (e.g., Compumedics, Emfit) specifying guaranteed replacement or repair within 2 weeks, with on-site spare units loaned during downtime, deferring redundancy cost to operational budget (~€30K/year) but accepting episodic brief gaps during unexpected failures or validation periods.

Adopt a tiered hardware strategy: deploy durable, battle-tested PSG electrodes (silver-silver chloride with alcohol-prep, cost +€15/participant) for the three scheduled enhanced nights, and use cost-optimized dry-electrode headbands only for low-burden nightly monitoring, enabling headband replacement without PSG-grade validation, reducing redundancy cost to ~€60K but requiring dual technician training protocols.
review: Controls upfront capital investment vs. operational service dependency and just-in-time replacement risk. Weakness: The options fail to account for the learning curve required when switching between sensor brands or models mid-project (e.g., if a vendor discontinues a product, external suppliers may introduce subtle impedance or noise characteristics that confound algorithm training data in Aim 3).

name: Ethics Amendment and Regulatory Agility Framework
consequences: Immediate: The facility operates under a single ethics approval from the University of Bonn's Institutional Review Board (Ethikkommission) issued at project launch (month 0), covering the three aims, recruitment protocol, and data-sharing policy. Systemic: German regulatory bodies (Ethics Commission, Data Protection Authority, Hospital Hygiene Inspectorate) may issue mid-project guidance (e.g., new GDPR interpretations on video anonymization, novel safety standards for residential sleep monitoring, or updates to AASM criteria impacting inclusion/exclusion logic) that require protocol amendments; each amendment incurs a 6–12 week ethics review cycle, delaying participant enrollment by 1–2 cohorts (4–8 weeks) and consuming 40–60 hours of PI coordination work per amendment. Strategic: Regulatory agility directly impacts recruitment velocity (Lever 2) and year-2 enrollment targets; if even one major amendment occurs months 9–12, the facility falls behind enrollment pace, risking the month-18 failure-trigger threshold (<25 participants), which could terminate conditional year-3 funding even if scientific progress is sound.
options: File a comprehensive umbrella ethics amendment framework at project launch, pre-emptively addressing anticipated guidance evolution (video de-identification standards, escalation criteria for unsafe behavior, data retention timelines), securing ethics board agreement for fast-track amendments under pre-specified conditions, reducing mid-project review cycles from 8–12 weeks to 2–3 weeks.

Establish a standing Regulatory Liaison Committee (PI, Ethics Officer from University Hospital Bonn, external Ethics Board representative, Data Protection Officer) meeting quarterly, proactively monitoring German regulatory landscape and publishing interpretive guidance internal to the facility, enabling rapid micro-amendments (<4 weeks) without full ethics re-review for procedural refinements while preserving formal review for substantive protocol changes.

Maintain a strict protocol-adherence model with zero mid-project amendments, documenting all deviation requests and deferring them to a phase-two protocol revision (month 28–32), accepting slower initial responsiveness to regulatory feedback but reducing administrative overhead by ~80 hours/year and providing a consolidated amendment package that demonstrates mature scientific governance to phase-two funders.
review: Controls regulatory responsiveness vs. administrative burden and enrollment continuity. Weakness: The options fail to consider how regional variations across German states (some Länder may have stricter video-retention rules) affect multi-site replication, or whether the external Ethics Board representative on a standing committee creates conflicts of interest in reviewing facility-initiated amendments.

name: Publication Sequencing and Competitive Grant Narrative Alignment
consequences: Immediate: The facility can generate publishable data along three timelines: (a) rapid methods papers (months 12–15) on facility design and recruitment feasibility, reaching small specialist audiences; (b) mid-term phenotyping reports (months 20–26) on Aim 2 longitudinal characterization with 30–40 captured events, offering moderate clinical impact; (c) mature longitudinal studies (months 28–36) integrating Aim 3 algorithm benchmarks and full cohort datasets, achieving higher citation potential. Systemic: A 'publish fast, publish often' strategy (3–4 papers by month 24) establishes proof-of-concept early, enabling rapid phase-two grant submissions by month 22–24, but risks preliminary or underpowered findings (e.g., <50 events per phenotype, algorithm validation on <80 positive cases) that invite critical peer-review feedback and reduce phase-two funding competitiveness; conversely, deferring publication to month 28–36 yields a single comprehensive dataset paper and two high-impact methodology papers but delays phase-two grant submission to month 28, compressing the funding-decision window to month 34–36 and risking a 6–12 month funding gap. Strategic: Publication timing directly shapes the facility's long-term trajectory—early publication attracts junior researcher collaborators and secondary-use interest (Lever 4) but commits the team to defending preliminary findings; delayed publication preserves scientific rigor but risks losing competitive advantage to other parasomnia centers (e.g., Stanford, Mayo Clinic, European initiatives) that publish methodologies first.
options: Execute a hybrid publication schedule: submit a methods-and-feasibility paper (Aims 1 and recruitment outcomes) by month 18, a preliminary phenotyping report (20–30 captured events, Aim 2) by month 24, and a final comprehensive dataset paper with algorithm benchmarks (Aim 3) by month 32, enabling phase-two grant submission at month 24 with interim data and month 32 with full validation, spreading review cycles across the timeline.

Consolidate into a single mature comprehensive publication (month 30) synthesizing all three aims, phenotypic characterization, and algorithm validation in a high-impact journal (Sleep, Journal of Neuroscience), deferring phase-two grant submission to month 28 but projecting greater citation impact and institutional prestige, acceptable only if DFG or internal funding includes a phase-2-contingency reserve preventing a funding cliff.

Prioritize rapid publication of the event-triage algorithm benchmarks (Aim 3, month 24–26) ahead of complete Aim 2 phenotyping, targeting machine-learning and clinical informatics journals to establish first-mover advantage in parasomnia automated detection, enabling phase-two framing as a clinical tool-validation study rather than fundamental phenotyping, at the cost of publishing phenotyping data 6–12 months later or as a secondary report.
review: Controls publication velocity and phase-2 grant competitiveness vs. statistical power and scientific credibility. Weakness: The options fail to address whether publishing preliminary (<50 event) datasets prematurely invites methodological criticism that undermines institutional reputation, or whether deferring publication risks that competitors with faster publication pipelines (e.g., international consortia) establish algorithmic primacy and crowd out secondary-use citations of the Bonn facility data.

name: University Hospital Bonn Integration and Institutional Bottleneck Mitigation
consequences: Immediate: The facility leverages University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology affiliation for clinical credibility, legal infrastructure, IT security, and collaborative scoring; this alignment enables rapid ethics approval, access to referral networks, and institutional data stewardship without building independent compliance functions. Systemic: Institutional dependencies create latent bottlenecks—if Hospital IT introduces mandatory infrastructure updates (e.g., data-center migration, security patches), the facility's NAS backup schedule may be disrupted for 2–4 weeks, delaying data availability for algorithm training (Aim 3); if the Hospital's legal team prioritizes other institutional contracts, ethics amendments (Lever 7) may experience queue delays of 4–6 weeks; if collaborative scoring at the Hospital sleep lab reaches >60 hours/week, the facility may face deprioritization relative to the Hospital's clinical PSG volume. Strategic: These dependencies are not immediately visible but collectively reduce scheduling flexibility by ~30% and create risk of cascading delays (delayed ethics approval → delayed recruitment → missed year-2 enrollment targets → month-18 review trigger). Mitigation requires either early institutional alignment or selective independence in critical functions.
options: Formalize a nested Memorandum of Understanding with University Hospital Bonn specifying guaranteed service levels for ethics liaison (2-week turnaround on amendments), IT support (4-hour response SLA for data-center incidents, nightly backup completion by 06:00), and collaborative scoring (minimum 40 hours/week reserved capacity), with escalation to Department Director if targets slip, at a cost of €50K/year in formal service-level agreement administration.

Establish a parallel institutional infrastructure: retain Hospital affiliation for clinical credibility and recruitment referrals, but procure independent network management (dedicated IT contractor, €35K/year), hire an in-house Data Protection Officer (0.3 FTE, €18K/year), and contract independent raters outside the Hospital system (external sleep labs or trained freelance scorers, €25K/year), reducing dependency on Hospital bottlenecks but fragmenting institutional alignment and risking loss of referral network advantage.

Negotiate a tiered integration model: use Hospital infrastructure for high-stakes functions (ethics liaison, legal review, IT security audits) but establish a dedicated Facility Operations Officer (0.8 FTE, €40K/year, hired by the facility but embedded in Hospital) responsible for internal scheduling, bottleneck escalation, and inter-departmental coordination, creating a buffer layer that translates institutional priorities into facility-specific service commitments.
review: Controls institutional prestige and resource access vs. operational independence and schedule reliability. Weakness: The options fail to account for whether University Hospital Bonn's clinical leadership (Department Director, hospital administration) remains stable over a 3-year cycle, or whether leadership transitions could disrupt informal collaborative relationships and scheduled scoring agreements despite formal MOUs.

name: Phenotypic Characterization Scope and Phenotype-Algorithm Feedback Loop
consequences: Immediate: Aim 2 targets characterization of 'episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering' within a longitudinal 2–8 week observation window; this generates approximately 35–56 adjudicated events (at 70–80% capture rate across 50–70 participants), sufficient to describe population-level distributions (mean frequency, arousal-stage ratios) but insufficient for within-phenotype stratification (e.g., distinguishing 'stereotyped confusional arousals with sleep-talking' from 'chaotic sleep terrors with variable motor behavior'). Systemic: If algorithm training (Aim 3) uses aggregated, undifferentiated parasomnia events as the positive class, model performance plateaus at 80–83% sensitivity due to heterogeneous feature distributions; conversely, if phenotypic subtyping (motor complexity, vocalization presence, EEG morphology) is implemented post-hoc, semi-automated classification requires retraining on 'phenotype-stratified' event pools, extending algorithm development by 4–6 weeks and pushing final Aim 3 publication to month 32–34. Strategic: The interplay between Aim 2 (phenotyping granularity) and Aim 3 (algorithm specificity) is a critical design choice—superficial Aim 2 work enables fast Aim 3 delivery but produces low-utility tools (generic parasomnia detector with 80% sensitivity); detailed Aim 2 work (10+ phenotypic subgroups) enables high-utility tools (phenotype-specific detectors) but requires extended algorithm validation and delays phase-two grant submission.
options: Implement a minimal phenotypic taxonomy: three categorical classes (NREM parasomnia with prominent motor behavior, NREM parasomnia with prominent vocalization, mixed/undifferentiated), annotated prospectively by technicians on all captured events, enabling stratified algorithm training by month 24–26 and publication of phenotype-specific sensitivity/specificity estimates, accepted as publishable Aim 3 outcome by month 28.

Conduct an exploratory post-hoc clustering analysis (month 20–22) using unsupervised machine learning (k-means, hierarchical clustering) on multimodal features (EEG spectral signature, motor variability, arousal architecture, temporal patterns within episodes), derive a data-driven phenotypic taxonomy (5–8 clusters), then retrain algorithm on clustered subsets, accepting a longer development cycle (algorithm paper month 32–34) but generating a novel, empirically-grounded nosology for parasomnia phenotypes.

Defer detailed phenotypic characterization to phase-two as a separate research aim, focusing Aim 2 on population-level descriptors (frequency, temporal clustering, trigger associations) and training a single generalist algorithm (Aim 3) on undifferentiated events, accepting 78–82% sensitivity but enabling rapid phase-two proposal development by month 24 with explicit commitment to 'phenotype-stratified algorithm refinement' as a phase-two objective.
review: Controls Aim 2-Aim 3 scientific integration and algorithm utility vs. timeline compression and phenotypic novelty. Weakness: The options fail to address whether the facility has sufficient expertise in unsupervised learning and parasomnia phenotyping to credibly execute a data-driven taxonomy, or whether publishing a novel phenotypic framework without external clinical validation invites skepticism from sleep medicine specialists who may view it as post-hoc rationalization rather than clinically meaningful stratification.

name: Sleep-Stage Temporal Sampling and Bias Mitigation in Event Capture
consequences: Immediate: The current enhanced-night PSG schedule (first night, mid-stay night, final night) uses a fixed calendar sampling frame that may not align with each participant's parasomnia chronotype; if a participant's episodes preferentially occur during early-night stage N3 deep sleep (baseline night 1) or late-night post-REM transitions (night 14), but enhanced PSG occurs on nights 4, 15, and 28, the enhanced montage captures noise-heavy transitions but misses the participant's true high-risk windows. Systemic: This mismatch introduces a systematic bias in Aim 2 phenotypic characterization—reported episode morphology, arousal structure, and EEG spectral signatures may overrepresent rare behavioral variants (sleep-stage edge cases) while underrepresenting the participant's stereotyped dominant phenotype; at a population level, 25–30% of reported Aim 2 phenotypes may be artifacts of sampling schedule rather than true individual differences, inflating observed heterogeneity and reducing between-person statistical power by ~15–20%. Strategic: Biased Aim 2 characterization weakens Aim 3 (algorithm training on inflated phenotypic noise) and undermines phase-two claims about 'validated longitudinal phenotyping,' potentially triggering external review criticism that the facility conflated sampling artifacts with true phenotypic variation.
options: Implement adaptive enhanced-night scheduling: conduct low-burden nightly EEG+actigraphy during the full baseline week, identify each participant's episode-prone sleep-stage window (e.g., cumulative stage N3 residence time, REM episode clustering), then schedule enhanced PSG nights to bracket that window plus alternating control nights, requiring manual sleep-stage analysis (8–12 hours per participant) but yielding temporally-representative phenotypic data and reducing Aim 2 characterization bias below 8%.

Standardize a fixed-window enhanced-night schedule (nights 2–3, 7–8, 14–15, 21–22, 27–28) that systematically samples early-night, mid-cycle, and late-stay periods, accepting known sampling bias but enabling pooled analysis across participants via statistical harmonization and explicit documentation of the bias direction in methods sections, simplifying operations but publishing transparent limitations.

Deploy continuous PSG montage (EEG, EOG, EMG) on all nights concurrent with low-burden sensors, reducing the distinction between 'enhanced' and 'baseline' nights and capturing all episodes at clinical-grade resolution, accepting a 40–50% increase in year-1 equipment cost (€180–220K) and ongoing electrode/consumable expenses (€120K/year), but eliminating temporal sampling bias entirely and enabling real-time algorithm training on complete temporal coverage.
review: Controls temporal sampling representativeness vs. operational complexity and equipment cost. Weakness: The options fail to account for whether participants' sleep-stage architecture changes across a 2–8 week stay due to facility habituation, sleep debt accumulation, or medication effects, rendering early-week sleep-stage characterization invalid for predicting late-week episode timing.

name: Collateral-Informant Data Integration and Ecological Validity Enhancement
consequences: Immediate: Current protocol incorporates collateral history at pre-admission screening (bed partner or household member interview) but treats it as a one-time confirmation tool, not an ongoing data stream; during the residential stay, collateral informants (family members, roommates, or designated household proxies) are not engaged, forfeiting rich contextual data on real-world triggers (stressors, sleep environment disruptions, social interactions), episode severity perception (from observer standpoint), and behavioral nuances not captured by in-facility technician observation. Systemic: Omitting ongoing collateral data introduces an ecological validity gap—in-facility observed episodes may differ from real-world parasomnias due to absence of natural stressors, familiar sleep partners, and domestic environmental triggers; this gap directly biases Aim 2 trigger characterization (estimated bias of 20–30% for stress-related clustering and 15–25% for social-context effects), reducing external validity of identified patterns and weakening phase-two intervention claims ('if triggers identified in the facility do not match real-world triggers, interventions may be misaligned'). Strategic: Closing the ecological gap requires formalizing collateral-informant engagement (weekly remote interviews, incident-log submissions) during the stay, adding ~3–4 hours per participant per week in coordination overhead but yielding dual-perspective phenotyping (clinician-observed + informant-reported) that substantially strengthens Aim 2 credibility and phase-two intervention design.
options: Establish a structured collateral-informant protocol: recruit a designated household informant (bed partner, family member, or close contact) at baseline who provides weekly 20-minute remote interviews (via phone or secure video) during the participant's stay, reporting observed behaviors before facility admission, current well-being perception, and anticipated triggers; compile into a semi-structured incident log, analyzed in conjunction with in-facility video-audio records, at a coordination cost of ~€8K/year (0.2 FTE coordinator time).

Implement a real-time mobile EMA (ecological momentary assessment) app deployed to household informants, requesting brief daily notifications (morning and evening) on participant sleep quality, emotional stress, sleep environment factors, and any recalled behaviors from the prior night; aggregate EMA data with facility-captured events to identify contextual clustering patterns, requiring app development (€25K one-time) and ongoing technical support (€5K/year), but enabling quantified trigger association analysis.

Defer collateral integration to a post-hoc qualitative phase: retain baseline collateral history and conduct retrospective semi-structured interviews with informants after the participant's stay, focusing on ecological validity validation and trigger phenomenology without real-time data streams, reducing operational overhead to ~€3K/year but sacrificing prospective trigger-tracking and contemporaneous context documentation.
review: Controls ecological validity and trigger characterization richness vs. collateral-informant coordination burden and privacy/consent complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether collateral informants—often family members already stressed by the participant's parasomnia—experience interview fatigue or resentment at being enrolled in a research study tangentially, potentially introducing reporting bias or dropout, nor do they address liability if a collateral informant becomes aware of algorithmically-detected high-risk behaviors and feels obligated to intervene or disclose.

name: Facility Replicability Roadmap and Multi-Center Protocol Standardization
consequences: Immediate: The Bonn facility is designed as a bespoke residential research unit optimized for German institutional context, regulatory environment, and regional participant demographics; scientific publications will describe methods, but without explicit replicability packaging, other European sleep centers face 12–18 month lead time to adapt the protocol, train staff, and validate equivalence across sites. Systemic: If phase-two funding (conditional at month 24) mandates validation or expansion to 2–3 additional European sites (e.g., Munich, Amsterdam, Paris), the absence of a pre-designed replicability roadmap introduces a 'late-stage standardization burden'—each site must independently implement equipment, staffing, ethics, and operational protocols, consuming 200–300 hours of cross-site coordination work and risking de facto protocol drift (different inclusion thresholds, equipment variants, annotation practices) that undermines meta-analytic pooling of data across sites. Strategic: Early investment in replicability infrastructure (detailed standard operating procedures, staff training templates, equipment procurement specifications, ethics amendment templates) at months 0–6 requires ~80–120 hours of PI and coordinator time but enables rapid multi-center deployment at months 24–30, positioning the Bonn facility as a 'methodology hub' and substantially increasing phase-two funding appeal (grant agencies favor consortial models with clear scalability).
options: Develop a comprehensive Facility Replication Toolkit (months 6–12) including: detailed standard operating procedures (SOP binder, 200+ pages), staff training curriculum with video modules, equipment procurement guidance (vendor, model numbers, setup checklists), ethics template amendments for German and non-German contexts, data-harmonization protocols for multi-center EEG/sensor alignment, and a 'replicability readiness checklist' enabling external sites to self-assess equivalence; distribute via open-access documentation platform (e.g., OSF, Zenodo) by month 12, at a development cost of €35K and ongoing maintenance of €5K/year.

Designate the Bonn facility as a 'flagship-only' model and structure phase-two funding around a consortium-capacity-building approach: instead of full replication, partner sites conduct limited algorithm-validation studies using de-identified Bonn data and their own smaller participant cohorts, avoiding the burden of residential facility replication but limiting multi-center sample sizes and reducing opportunities for network-effect data pooling.

Negotiate a formal multi-center consortium agreement (months 12–18) with 2–3 partner sleep centers (Munich, Amsterdam, or Paris) that commit to site-specific feasibility studies (months 20–24) with targeted funding to adapt rather than fully replicate, establishing harmonized core protocols (EEG electrode placement, PSG scoring criteria, recruitment age-range) while permitting local variation in facility design and staffing, enabling phased multi-center launch at month 28–30 without demanding full protocol fidelity.
review: Controls phase-two expansion readiness and consortium appeal vs. early operational overhead and risk of premature standardization. Weakness: The options fail to address whether regulatory heterogeneity across European jurisdictions (German ethics standards vs. Dutch data-privacy requirements vs. French institutional review timelines) permits meaningful 'harmonization' or whether genuine multi-center alignment requires parallel ethics approval processes that negate time savings.

name: Longitudinal Data Quality and Algorithm Robustness Under Missing Data Scenarios
consequences: Immediate: The facility plans continuous data collection across 2–8 week stays with nightly backup and redundant sensing streams (EEG, mattress, microphone, video); this assumes near-zero missing-data rates and uniform data quality across the stay. Systemic: In practice, participants experience technical failures (headband malfunction, mattress sensor dropout, network outages), equipment maintenance windows (monthly calibration, firmware updates), and behavioral non-compliance (participants removing uncomfortable headbands during deep sleep, disabling microphones due to privacy concerns); empirical experience across polysomnography centers suggests 5–12% of nights experience partial data loss in at least one sensor stream. If Aim 3 algorithm training and Aim 2 cross-individual phenotypic comparisons assume complete datasets, applying the trained model to real-world deployment data (with inherent 5–12% missingness) produces a 6–9% performance degradation (sensitivity drops from 85% to 78–80%, specificity from 94% to 88–90%), unacceptable for clinical decision support. Strategic: Proactive missing-data handling (imputation methods, sensitivity analyses, robustness testing) during algorithm development (Aim 3) costs ~30–40 additional hours of data-engineering work but ensures reproducible performance across deployment scenarios, strengthening phase-two grant framing and reducing risk of algorithm failure in real-world settings.
options: Implement a missing-data-robust algorithm framework: train the Aim 3 model on intentionally degraded datasets (randomly drop 5%, 10%, 15% of features per night) in parallel with the complete-data version, measure performance degradation curves, and select a final model that maintains >82% sensitivity and >92% specificity even under 10% missingness, explicitly documenting robustness bounds in publication and phase-two proposals.

Establish a data-quality assurance protocol with automated flags for missing-data incidents: technicians are alerted in real-time when any sensor stream drops >3% of expected data points, trigger manual review to determine if failure is technical (device malfunction requiring replacement) or behavioral (participant non-compliance requiring gentle re-education), enabling prospective intervention and reducing empirical missingness to <3%, simplifying algorithm robustness requirements but adding ~5 hours/week technician workload.

Adopt a modular algorithm architecture where core features (low-burden EEG and actigraphy) are sufficient for 80–83% sensitivity with 92% specificity, and optional enhanced features (EMG, microphone) boost performance to 85–88% sensitivity only when present; train separate models for 'baseline-data' and 'enhanced-data' scenarios, enabling deployment to diverse clinical settings with varying sensor availability, at the cost of added model complexity and validation burden.
review: Controls algorithm reliability under real-world deployment conditions vs. training-data complexity and validation scope. Weakness: The options fail to address whether the facility's missing-data mechanisms (equipment failure vs. behavioral dropout vs. technical maintenance) introduce systematically non-random patterns (e.g., participants who are most anxious or have severe parasomnias are more likely to remove uncomfortable sensors), violating missing-completely-at-random assumptions and biasing imputation or sensitivity analyses.

name: Long-Term Participant Follow-Up and Phase-Two Longitudinal Continuity
consequences: Immediate: The current protocol defines participant engagement as the residential 2–8 week stay (plus 1–2 week extensions for zero-event admissions); discharge includes de-briefing, clinical feedback on captured events, and data archival, but no systematic post-discharge follow-up. Systemic: Without longitudinal continuity post-discharge, the facility loses opportunity to assess real-world outcome trajectories (do in-facility behavioral insights translate to at-home behavior change? Do participants benefit from receiving de-identified event summaries and clinical phenotyping feedback?). This gap undermines phase-two intervention narratives—if the facility cannot demonstrate that Aim 2 phenotyping produces clinically actionable insights or that identified triggers remain stable post-discharge, intervention trials appear poorly motivated. Additionally, a 3–6 month post-discharge actigraphy or remote EMA follow-up (at negligible cost) would validate whether facility-observed parasomnia frequency and trigger clustering persist in real-world settings, addressing the ecological validity concern raised in Lever 12. Strategic: Extending participant engagement to 3–6 month post-discharge follow-up via low-burden remote data collection (monthly actigraphy shipment, quarterly EMA questionnaires, consent for re-contact if rare-event episodes occur) costs €15–25K total across the 50–70 participant cohort but generates phase-two evidence of external validity, strengthens intervention proposals, and enables retention of participants for future treatment-validation studies.
options: Implement a post-discharge remote monitoring protocol: participants receive a pre-programmed actigraphy watch and a brief monthly online questionnaire (5 minutes, querying sleep quality, episodes, triggers, and self-perceived behavior change) for 6 months post-discharge; analysis focuses on comparing post-discharge parasomnia frequency and trigger clustering to in-facility observations, cost €20K total, and findings published as a secondary 'real-world validation' paper by month 28–30.

Establish a post-discharge participant advisory panel: interested participants (target 15–20 of 50–70) are invited to join quarterly video calls with the research team to discuss facility findings, receive clinical summaries of their own captured events, and provide qualitative feedback on behavior-change outcomes; use these interviews as rich qualitative validation of Aim 2 phenotyping and phase-two intervention design, at a cost of €3K/year coordination time, generating high-value narrative evidence for grant proposals.

Defer post-discharge follow-up to phase-two as an explicit longitudinal study aim: at discharge, all participants consent to optional re-contact for future protocol participation; use phase-two funding to conduct a 12-month prospective follow-up cohort study tracking Aim 2 phenotypic stability and real-world outcomes, avoiding additional year-1–3 resource demands but deferring external-validity evidence to phase-two and accepting a 6-month gap in longitudinal outcome data for phase-two grant submission planning.
review: Controls real-world outcome validation and phase-two evidence generation vs. resource allocation to current-phase research activities. Weakness: The options fail to address whether post-discharge participants experience parasomnia symptom changes due to placebo effects of being monitored and receiving detailed feedback (meta-awareness), which could inflate perceived behavior change and confound attributions about trigger modification or natural history trajectories.
